segments.s621-g25: به رزم اندرون is a fronted prepositional phrase meaning “into the interior/heat of the battle.” او is the personal pronoun subject (“he”), followed by the predicate گرفتارد شد, where گرفتار is a predicative adjective/participle “caught/trapped” and شد is the past-tense copula/auxiliary “became/was,” so the clause reads literally “into the battle’s interior he became trapped.”
segments.s621-g26: وزو is the fused sequence و + زو (classical/poetic: ‘and from/for him’), introducing the next clause; چرخ گردنده is a nominative phrase “the revolving wheel” (a poetic image for the sky/fate), and بیزار شد uses the adjective بیزار “averse/hostile” with شد “became,” so the clause means “and the turning wheel became averse to him.”
segments.s621-g27: کنون is an adverb “now.” بسته is a predicative/adjectival participle “bound/tied,” placed before the verb for emphasis. آوردمش is the past tense “I brought” with the object clitic -ش “him” attached; بر هیون is the prepositional phrase “onto/ upon the beast.” Word order (adverb — modifier — verb+clitic — prepositional phrase) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s621-g28: جگر خسته is a nominal phrase where جگر (here figurative) means the seat of feeling/heart and خسته is the adjective “tired/weary.” و simply coordinates, and دیدگان پر ز خون is a prepositional/nominal phrase: دیدگان “the eyes,” پر “full (of),” ز (poetic form of از) “of/from,” and خون “blood,” so together “his heart weary and his eyes full of blood.”
segments.s621-g29: همه is “all/everyone.” گردن سرکشان literally ‘the necks of the rebellious’ (سرکشان = those who are proud/rebellious) where the genitive relation is implicit. گشت is the past “became/turned,” and نرم is the adjective “soft/pliant,” so the clause means their necks grew pliant/submissive. (سرکشان is a plural/attributive form referring to rebellious people.)
segments.s621-g30: زبان چرب is a compact noun+adjective phrase where زبان refers to speech/tongue and چرب (literally “oily”) idiomatically means “flattering.” و links the next phrase; دلها پر از خون گرم: دلها “the hearts,” پر از “full of,” خون “blood,” and گرم here qualifies خون as “warm/hot” in the sense of fervent/ardent, so “their tongues flattering and their hearts full of warm/blood-hot passion.”
segments.s621-g31: پذیرفت is the simple past “accepted/took.” باژ is the noun “cry/shout,” and the following آنک بدخواه بود is a relative clause modifying باژ: آنک “who/that,” بدخواه “an ill-wisher/enemy” and بود the past copula “was.” Syntactically the verb takes باژ (modified by the relative clause) as its object: “it accepted the cry which belonged to an ill‑wisher.”
segments.s621-g32: به راه آمدند is a verbal phrase with به راه “to the way/path” and آمدند “they came” (3rd pl. past), meaning “they came to the right path” or “they were brought onto the way.” آنک introduces the relative clause referring to those people, and بی‌راه بود (“were off the way/astray”) describes them prior to coming; بود is the past copula. The overall meaning: those who had been astray came to the path.
segments.s621-g9: This is a nominal phrase headed by خداوند (here an honorific 'lord/sovereign') followed by two nouns linked by و. پیروزی and دستگاه are attributes of that lord: 'lord of victory and (lord) of the court.' Persian allows a head noun (خداوند) to govern a series of genitival or attributive nouns that follow it without any linking preposition. The coordinating و simply joins the two attributes.
segments.s621-g10: The phrase repeats the honorific خداوند and then lists three proper/celestial names joined by و: بهرام (here the planetary/heroic Mars), کیوان (Saturn), and ماه (the Moon). The repetition of خداوند functions rhetorically (vocative/epithet) and the coordinate list names the powers invoked; there is no verb — a stock poetic nominal enumeration.
segments.s621-g11: خداوند is again the head; گردنده is a present/agentive participle (the one who turns) modifying an omitted verbal idea and attaching to چرخ بلند ('the lofty wheel' = heaven/sky or cosmic wheel). Persian places the participle after the head noun to qualify its action here ('the lord turning the high wheel').
segments.s621-g12: Another nominal clause with خداوند plus a past/result participle ارمنده ('laid to rest' / 'causing to rest') followed by the object/phrase خاک نژند ('base/wretched earth'). The participle stands in place of a fuller verb phrase (he who lays low / puts to rest the lowly earth), with the copula omitted as normal in this poetic listing.
segments.s621-g13: بزرگی و خردی is a coordinated subject ('great and small' / 'greatness and smallness'); به پیمان اوست uses به with a nominal phrase to indicate agency or provenance ('by his covenant' / 'according to his decree'), and ست is the copula 'is' (poetically attached as اوست). Word order is Subject + Circumstantial phrase + copula.
segments.s621-g14: همه بودنی is a noun phrase 'all that is' or 'all beings'; زیر فرمان اوست places that phrase 'under his command' (زیر = 'under/subject to', فرمان = 'command'), followed by the copula اوست. This is a standard Persian construction: Topic + locative/relational phrase + copula.
segments.s621-g15: نوشتم is the 1st-person singular past tense 'I wrote.' یکی نامه is an indefinite object 'a certain/one letter' (یکی used here as an indefinite article), and از مرز چین is a prepositional phrase 'from the frontier/border of China.' Persian commonly orders verb + object + source in narrative past-tense clauses like this.
segments.s621-g16: به نزد is a fixed locative/prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of' (here 'to' someone). برادر is the indirect object 'brother' (addressee). The following به ایران‌زمین specifies the destination/land ('to the land of Iran'); the second به expresses direction/toward. The sequence gives recipient/location after the written object.
segments.s621-g17: به نزد ... is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to / in the presence of'. Here به (to) + نزد (in the presence of / to) mark the addressees; بزرگان ایرانیان is the nominal phrase 'the great ones / chiefs of the Iranians' (بزرگان = 'great ones', ایرانیان = 'Iranians'). The whole phrase indicates the recipients of the action that follows.
segments.s621-g18: نوشتن here functions as the verbal action 'wrote / sent' in an epic/poetic register (the infinitive/masdar form is used to state the action). همین 'this very' modifies the implied نامه ('this very letter'). نامه‌بر + پرنیان is best read as 'a silken courier' / 'by a courier in silk' (پرنیان 'silken' qualifies نامه‌بر 'letter‑carrier'), so the phrase means 'sent this same letter by a silken courier.'
segments.s621-g19: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause: 'whoever / anyone who'. که is the relativizer ('who/that'). او is the third‑person pronoun 'he/that' used here to refer back to the person; رزم خاقان is a genitive construction 'the battle of the khagan' (ezāfe omitted in poetry). ندید is past negative 'did not see.' Together: 'whoever did not see the khagan's battle.'
segments.s621-g20: ازین جنگجویان = 'from these warriors' (از + این with the clitic form 'ین'). بباید + شنید is a modal construction: بباید 'one must / should' + the masdar/infinitive شنید 'hear / learn (of it)'. Literarily: 'one should hear (about it) from these warriors.'
segments.s621-g21: سپه بود = 'there was an army / the host was' (بود = past copula). چندانک = 'to such an extent / so much so' and گفتی here is a parenthetical 'you might say / it is said' used to introduce the simile; سپهر 'the sky' is the noun picked up by the following clause. The segment sets up a comparison: 'The host was so great, one might say, (that) the sky ...'.
segments.s621-g22: ز گردش = 'from (the) turning / from the rotation' (ز = from, گردش = rotation/circuit). به قیر اندر اندود چهر is an older/poetic word order meaning 'the face (چهر) was smeared (اندر اندود, literally 'in/into coated') with tar (به قیر)'. Note that اندود is a participial/adjectival perfect 'coated/smeared' and the phrase describes the sky's face being blackened with tar by the turning (i.e. the turmoil).
segments.s621-g23: همه مرز شد = 'the whole / entire frontier became' (همه = entire, مرز = border/frontier, شد = became). همچو دریای خون = 'like a sea of blood' (همچو = like; دریای خون = sea of blood). This is a straightforward simile: the border turned into a sea of blood.
segments.s621-g24: سر بخت = literally 'the head of fortune' (a poetic expression for 'good fortune' or 'the chief part of fate'). بیداد گشته is a verbal phrase 'has become one of oppression / injustice' (بیداد = oppression/woe; گشته = become/turned). نگون = 'overthrown / ill‑fated / brought low.' Together the line says that fortune's head was turned into oppression and brought low—i.e., luck/fortune is defeated and miserable.
segments.s621-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; شد is a past verb here ('became' / 'came to be') that head the clause. کار is the noun 'affair/matter' and is modified by the following phrase توران زمین ساخته, where ساخته is a past participle meaning 'made/settled' describing کار; Persian poetic word order places the modifier after the noun rather than using a separate relative clause.
segments.s621-g2: This is an independent clause: دل شاه = 'the king's heart' (noun + possessor), ز + اندیشه = 'from/because of thought/worry' with ز as the topical/prepositional marker, and پرداخته is a perfective/participle form indicating the state/result ('has departed / been freed / ceased to be occupied by'). The whole means the king's heart was relieved of anxious thought.
segments.s621-g3: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces the subordinate clause 'that / so that', پیش او = 'in his presence / before him', and شد دبیر is a verbal phrase 'a scribe came / was made (to come)'; the sequence reflects older/poetic Persian inversion where the verb may follow the object and subject.
segments.s621-g4: قلم خواست is a simple past clause 'he requested/desired a pen' (قلم = pen; خواست = wanted/requested). با introduces accompanying items ('with'), here مشک ('musk') و چینی حریر is a coordinated noun phrase 'and Chinese silk' (چینی = porcelain/Chinese; حریر = silk), listing what he asked to be provided.
segments.s621-g5: This clause is ordered in a literary way: به نرسی = 'to Nersi' (indirect object), یکی نامه = 'a certain letter' (object), فرمود = 'he composed/ordered/sent' (past verb), and شاه placed at the end is the subject. The verb فرمود here functions as 'wrote/sent/made' in the context of dispatching a letter.
segments.s621-g6: ز introduces the topic/reason 'about/concerning'; پیکار ترکان = 'the fighting/battle with the Turks' (پیکار = battle, ترکان = the Turks), and و کار سپاه = 'and the affairs of the army' with کار as 'affair' and سپاه 'army'. Together this gives the subjects of the letter.
segments.s621-g7: سر نامه = 'the head/opening of the letter' (سر used for 'beginning/top'), کرد is the past verb 'he made/put', and آفرین نهان is a noun phrase 'a hidden praise' or 'secret acclamation' placed as the salutation/heading. The sequence indicates he set this phrase at the letter's opening.
segments.s621-g8: ازین (here written as از + ین) = 'from this', بنده = 'servant' (a humble self-reference), بر = 'to/toward' used as the preposition for addressee, کردگار = 'the Creator/God', and جهان = 'the world'. Together they form the salutation 'From this servant to the Creator of the world.'
segments.s621-g33: This is a pre-verbal temporal and circumstantial phrase. کنون (now) sets the time; از پس نامه من literally 'from after my letter' = 'after my letter' (از ... پس is a poetic expression meaning 'following'). با سپاه is an appended prepositional phrase 'with an army' that will attach to the verb that follows (it is not the main verb). Word order here places time and circumstances before the verb, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s621-g34: بیایم is a present-subjunctive/optative form used here in narrative to express a future or jussive sense — 'I shall / I will come' or 'let me come'; it functions as the sentence's main verb. به کام دل نیک‌خواه is a dative/benefactive phrase: به کام = 'to the satisfaction/fulfilment of', دل نیک‌خواه = 'the heart of the well-wisher' (دل + postpositive noun نیک‌خواه = 'the well-wisher's heart'). The whole phrase indicates purpose/result: he comes in order to gratify the well-wisher's desire.
segments.s621-g35: This noun phrase contains the plural noun هیونان 'beasts/steeds' followed by two adjectival compounds that qualify it. کفک‌افگن is a compound adjective 'foam-throwing / foam-flecked' (picture of animals foaming at the mouth), and بادپای is another compound adjective 'wind-footed / swift-footed'. In Persian poetic style adjectives often follow the noun they modify.
segments.s621-g36: برفتند is the past third-person plural narrative verb 'they went / they departed / they rushed off'. چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by ابر غران 'a roaring cloud' (ابر modified by غران 'roaring'), and ز جای uses the poetic preposition ز (from) + جای (place) = 'from their place / from their position'. The clause describes sudden movement compared by a vivid natural image.
segments.s621-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه is the subject 'the letter'; به نزدیک means 'near / close'; نرسی and رسید are two verbal forms from رسیدن used in sequence for emphasis: نرسی (an approaching/attaining form) + رسید (arrived) — together 'drew near and came' (a common poetic doubling to indicate approach then arrival). This double-verb construction intensifies the action.
segments.s621-g38: ز شادی = 'from joy / because of joy'. دل پادشا 'the king's heart' is the grammatical object/topic, and بردمید is a past verb meaning literally 'carried off / was lifted away' used metaphorically: 'his heart was carried away (with joy)' = 'his heart leapt/was overjoyed'. The verb is pictorial/archaic and functions transitively in form but here describes an affective surge.
segments.s621-g39: بشد here is a narrative past/inchoative 'it happened / came to be / came' — used to mark the appearance or arrival of someone. موبد موبدان is a titulary doubling meaning 'mōbed of mōbeds' = 'chief priest'; موبدان as a reiteration intensifies/title. پیش + اوی = 'before him / in his presence', where اوی is an emphatic/archaic third-person clitic referring back to the king. The whole clause says the chief priest came into the king's presence.
segments.s621-g40: This is a relative clause modifying an implied group: هرانکس 'whosoever' + که 'who' + بود past 'was' + از یلان 'of the heroes' describes membership. جنگ جوی is a postpositive adjective phrase: جنگ 'war/battle' + جوی 'seeking' (a productive adjectival/agentive suffix) = 'war-seeking' or 'eager for fight'. Together: 'whoever, among the champions, was eager for battle.'
segments.s621-g73: This is an asyndetic list of ethnonyms/nisbas (people from places or named groups). Each item is coordinated by و (and). چغانی and چگلی function as adjectival/tribal names (nisba-like), بلخی = “of Balkh.” ردان closes the phrase as a plural substantive labeling the group; the phrase as a whole names several contingents.
segments.s621-g74: Continues the enumeration: بخاری is another group-name (people of Bukhara) joined by و (and). از here introduces a source/association (“from”/“out of”) linking غرجگان (the clamour/roar) to موبدان (the mobeds, Zoroastrian priests); syntactically موبدان is governed by از + غرجگان.
segments.s621-g75: برفتند is simple past third‑person plural ‘they departed/went’. با باژ = ‘with a cry(s)’ (با + noun). The sequence و برسم به دست groups a prepositional/adverbial phrase: برسم here is the prefixed prepositional/nominal use meaning ‘with/by the custom/ritual’ (poetic بِرِسم), followed by به دست ‘in hand’ — together ‘with the ritual in hand’. Note: برسم is a poetic form (not the verb رسید).
segments.s621-g76: نیایش کنان is a circumstantial participial phrase: نیایش (prayer) + کنان (present‑participle/adverbial ‘doing’) = ‘praying’ or ‘in prayer’. پیش is a preposition/adverb ‘before/in the presence of’. آتش‌پرست is the noun ‘fire‑worshipper’; the whole phrase modifies برفتند telling how/where they went.
segments.s621-g77: که introduces reported speech/content (‘that…’). ما شاه را یکسره بنده‌ایم is a declarative clause: ما (we) + شاه را (to/the king, را marking the theme/indirect object) + یکسره (completely) + بنده‌ایم (we are servants; بنده used predicatively with the present plural ending ‑ایم). The clause states full submission to the king.
segments.s621-g78: همان picks out ‘that very (thing/cry)’. باژ را marks the cry as the direct/ topical object (را). گردن افگنده‌ایم is a compound predicate: گردن (neck) + افگنده (past participle of افکندن 'to cast/lower') + ایـم (we) — literally ‘we have cast our necks’, idiomatically ‘we have bowed/submit’. The perfective participle + personal ending yields a perfect/resultant sense.
segments.s621-g79: همان نیز = ‘that same, too/also’. هر سال = ‘each/ every year’ (هر + سال). با باژ و ساو = ‘with cries and Sāv’; ساو is a proper name/place here and coordinates with باژ by و. The whole segment sets a recurring annual action/context.
segments.s621-g80: به درگه شدی uses به + درگه (to/at the royal court) with شدی (poetic/archaic use of شدن ‘to come/to happen/turn up’), so: ‘came to the court’. هرک is an indefinite ‘whoever’; بودیش is an archaic form meaning ‘it was to him/it belonged to him’ (بود + enclitic form). تاو (radiance/glory) is the item assigned: ‘that radiance belonged to whoever it was.’ The clause states possession/assignment of the radiance to whichever person was present.
segments.s621-g41: به شادی is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'with joy' (the preposition به used here in the adverbial/“-ly” sense). برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز ایران ('from Iran') gives the source, and خروش is the noun 'a cry/uproar' that is presented as what arose; word order is poetic: adverbial + verb + source + noun.
segments.s621-g42: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they placed/put'. هر یک 'each one' is the subject; به آواز گوش is a prepositional phrase 'to/against the sound (voice)', here meaning 'they put their ear to the sound.' Note the literal 'put ear to the voice' construction for 'listen closely.'
segments.s621-g43: دل نامداران is a genitive-like expression 'the hearts of the renowned (men)'. ز here marks the cause/reason 'because of' and attaches to تشویر شاه 'the king's display/ornamentation' (تشویر followed by شاه as its possessor).
segments.s621-g44: همی بود is a continuous/iterative past ('were continually' / 'kept on being'). پیچان is a predicative adjective 'twisted, turned' describing the hearts. ز بهر گناه is a causal/purpose phrase 'for the sake of / because of sin' (ز marking cause and بهر giving the 'for the sake of' nuance).
segments.s621-g45: به پوزش is adverbial 'with apology/asking pardon' (به used in the adverbial/manner sense). به نزدیک موبد شدند is a movement/approach clause: به + نزدیک + موبد = 'toward/near the mobed', with شدند (past plural of شدن) 'they went/they came' or 'they became near' in a conventional sense 'they approached the mobed.'
segments.s621-g46: همه is 'everyone/all'. دل‌هراسان is a compound adjective 'heart-frightened, terrified'. ز هر بد marks the cause/object of fear 'of every ill/misfortune', and شدند (past plural) is the copula/verb 'became/were.'
segments.s621-g47: کز is a subordinating particle here ('that' / 'for that'), introducing the feared cause. اندیشه کژ is 'crooked thought' (noun + adjective), و فرمان دیو links the second agent 'and the demon's command' — both act as the agents that might mislead.
segments.s621-g48: ببرد is a volitional/potential present form 'may carry away / might take' (the verb برد with a prefix b- conveying potentiality here). دل is the object 'the heart'; از راه گیهان is the prepositional phrase 'from the way/path of the world'; خدیو is the possessor ('the khedive' — i.e. the ruler whose heart would be carried away). Word order is poetic: object + prepositional phrase + possessor.
segments.s621-g49: ب + دان here functions as the fused demonstrative/adverbial 'بدان' = 'by/with that'; مایه لشکر is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the resource/force of the army' (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe). که introduces a relative clause describing that مایه; برد is used here in a causative/result sense ('a gain/thing brought about') governing the object این گمان 'this belief'. The whole segment reads 'by that army‑resource which brought this belief'.
segments.s621-g50: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'; یزدان is the divine subject 'God'; گشاید is a classical present/future‑oriented verb 'opens' or 'will open' (literary optative/indicative sense); در آسمان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/within the heavens'. Together: 'that God opens in the heavens'.
segments.s621-g51: شگفتیست is the fixed predicative phrase 'it is marvelous'; این is the topical demonstrative 'this'; کز (classical contraction of 'که از') introduces the source/thing 'that/from which'; گمان is the noun 'conjecture/belief'; بگذرد is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'passes/exceeds' (literary infinitive/3rd‑person sense 'it surpasses'). The segment states the surprising fact: 'it is marvelous that this conjecture surpasses ...'.
segments.s621-g52: هم here means 'even' or 'also' adding emphasis ('even from'); از marks the source 'from'; رای is 'opinion/counsel/judgment'; داننده is an agentive noun 'one who knows' (i.e., a knowledgeable person); مرد خرد is a noun phrase 'man of wisdom' ('mard‑e kherad' — a wise man). Together: 'even from the judgment of a wise/knowing man.'
segments.s621-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; پاسخ is 'reply/answer'; شود is the literary copular/auxiliary 'is/comes' (here marking the arrival/occurrence of the reply); نامه‌بر is 'messenger/envoy' (object/head of the clause); خوب و زشت is the correlative pair 'good and bad' describing the reply. Word order is inverted: 'when reply becomes (is) — messenger — good and bad', i.e. 'when the messenger's reply, whether good or bad, is received'.
segments.s621-g54: همین is the demonstrative 'this very'; پوزش is 'apology/excuse'; ما 'we/our' marks the possessor; بباید is the modal 'must/ought to' (literary obligatory form); نوشت is the verb 'to write' (infinitive/imperative sense here: 'must be written' or 'we must write'). The clause: 'this very apology of ours must be written.'
segments.s621-g55: که‌گر is a fused conditional 'if' (literary); چند here means 'some' or 'a few' (qualifying an indefinite amount); رفت is the past/preterite 'left/vanished' (i.e., 'was removed'); از marks origin 'from'; برزگان are 'the great men/notables'; گناه is 'sin/guilt'. Together: 'if, however, some guilt departed from the nobles'.
segments.s621-g56: ببخشد is the present/subjunctive form of بخشیدن used here as 'will forgive' or 'would forgive'; مگر in this rhetorical context functions like a questioning/exclamatory particle ('would...?/could...?') rather than a plain 'except'; نامبردار qualifies شاه as 'the named/renowned' (epithet for the king); شاه is 'king'. The line asks rhetorically whether the famed king would forgive in that case.
segments.s621-g65: The correlative negation 'نه ... نه ...' lists two excluded causes: 'نه از دشمنی بد' and 'نه از درد و کین'. 'از' marks the source or cause ('out of / because of'), 'دشمنی' is the noun 'enmity' followed by the adjective 'بد' (typical Persian order noun + adjective), and 'درد و کین' is a coordinated noun phrase 'sorrow (pain) and hatred'.
segments.s621-g66: The clause continues the negation: 'نه بر شاه بودست کس را گزین'. 'نه' negates the whole clause; 'بر شاه' here means 'against/over the king'. 'بودست' is a poetic past copular/auxiliary 'was'; 'کس را' is the definite/accusative-marked object ('someone' + object marker 'را'); and 'گزین' (from گزیدن) is used as 'chosen'/'selected' (predicate). Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: literal 'not against the king was anyone chosen.'
segments.s621-g67: 'یکی' is the indefinite 'a certain' introducing an individual; 'مهتری' is a title/epithet meaning a senior/chief. 'نام او برزمهر' places the possessive ('his name') before the proper name (typical Persian order: 'name of him Barzmehr'). Together the phrase identifies 'a certain chief whose name was Barzmehr.'
segments.s621-g68: 'بدان' here is the compound 'به + آن' (to/unto that/him) literary/poetic use meaning 'to him' or 'toward that'; 'رفتن' is the verbal noun/infinitive 'going'; 'راه' is 'way/path'; 'بگشاد' is a prefixed past-form verb 'opened/revealed' (ب‑ + گشادن), and 'چهر' literally 'face/appearance' is used idiomatically to mean 'access/presence' or 'appearance before someone'. The phrase says he, by going to that man, opened the path of approach (i.e., gained access).
segments.s621-g69: 'بیامد' is the simple past 'came'. The prepositional phrase 'به نزدیک' means 'to/near', so 'بیامد به نزدیک شاه جهان' = 'he came close to the king of the world' ('شاه جهان' is an epithet 'king of the world'). Word order places the motion verb first followed by the location phrase.
segments.s621-g70: 'همه' quantifies the following noun 'رازها' (all the secrets). 'برگشاد' is a compound past verb 'opened/revealed' (بر + گشادن) meaning 'he disclosed', and 'از نهان' is an ablative phrase 'out of hiding / from the hidden'. Together: 'he revealed all the secrets from their concealment.'
segments.s621-g71: 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') marks cause: 'because of / from'. 'ز گفتار او' = 'by his speech'. 'شاه' is the subject, and 'خشنود گشت' is the verb phrase 'became pleased' (خشنود = pleased; گشت = became). The causal phrase precedes the subject for emphasis.
segments.s621-g72: 'چنین' = 'thus/so' comments on the result. 'آتش تیز' is a noun + adjective 'a keen/sharp fire' (آتش = fire, تیز = sharp/intense). 'بی‌دود' is a compound adjective 'without smoke / smokeless'. 'گشت' is again 'became'. The line uses a concise, metaphorical description: the fire (passion/anger) became sharp yet without smoke (i.e., controlled or purified).
segments.s621-g57: بپذرفت is a past, 3rd-person singular verb 'accepted' placed before the name (a common epic inversion). نرسی here is a proper name (not the verb 'to reach'), and که introduces the content of his acceptance (an indirect/quoted clause). ایدون is an archaic/poetic adverb rendered 'now' or 'at this point', and کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I should do / I will do' marking his intended action.
segments.s621-g58: که begins a subordinate/purpose clause 'so that/that'; کین here means 'hatred/vengeance' directed at the king; از + دل is the usual 'from the heart', شاه is the possessor 'the king', بیرون is the adverb 'out/away', and کنم is again 1st-person subjunctive/present 'I may make/do' — together expressing the purpose 'that I may drive the king's hatred out of his heart.'
segments.s621-g59: پس is a connective 'then/so'; آن نامه را is the definite object 'that letter' with the direct-object marker را; زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon'; and پاسخ نوشت is a verb phrase 'wrote a reply' (نوشت is past 3rd-person singular). Word order is literary: adverb and object framed before the verb.
segments.s621-g60: پدیدار کرد is a compound meaning 'made manifest / revealed' (پدیدار as 'manifest' + کرد 'made'); اندرو is a fused prepositional pronoun meaning 'in it / therein' referring back to the letter; خوب و زشت are the two items revealed ('the good and the bad'). The verb governs اندرو and the adjectives as its direct content.
segments.s621-g61: که is a connective introducing a description; ایرانیان is the plural subject 'the Iranians'; از پی is a fixed prepositional phrase 'after / in pursuit of'; درد و رنج are the objects 'pain and suffering' (درد used as general affliction here, رنج as 'suffering/hardship').
segments.s621-g62: همان here means 'the same / likewise'; از پی repeats the 'in pursuit of' pattern; بوم 'homeland', فرزند 'child/descendant', and گنج 'treasure' are the parallel items the Iranians are said to pursue. The clause contrasts two kinds of motives/aims.
segments.s621-g63: گرفتند is past plural 'they took/seized'; خاقان چین را is the object 'the Khagan of China' marked by را (so 'they took the Khagan of China'); پناه here is the noun 'refuge' — together expressing the idiom 'they took X as refuge' (i.e., they sought refuge with the Khagan of China).
segments.s621-g64: به نومیدی is a prepositional phrase 'in/with despair' (به + abstract noun); از indicates the cause 'from/of' and links to نامبردار شاه 'the aforementioned/renowned king' — the whole phrase explains that they sought the Khagan's protection because they had lost hope in that named king.

sentences.s621-s13: He was caught in the thick of battle, and the revolving wheel of fate turned against him.
sentences.s621-s14: Now I brought him, bound, upon the beast — his heart weary and his eyes full of blood.
sentences.s621-s15: All the rebels’ necks became soft; tongues were oily (flattering) and hearts full of warm blood.
sentences.s621-s16: It accepted the cry of him who was ill‑wishing; those who were astray came to the right way.
sentences.s621-s5: The lord of victory and of the court — the lord Bahram, Kayvān, and the Moon.
sentences.s621-s6: The lord who turns the lofty wheel; the lord who lays the base earth to rest.
sentences.s621-s7: Greatness and smallness are by his covenant; all that exists is under his command.
sentences.s621-s8: I wrote a letter from the border of China to my brother in the land of Iran.
sentences.s621-s9: He sent this same letter, by a silken courier, to the chiefs of the Iranians.
sentences.s621-s10: Whoever did not see the khagan's battle should learn of it from these warriors.
sentences.s621-s11: The host was so great, one might say, that the sky...
sentences.s621-s12: The whole frontier became like a sea of blood; fortune's head was turned to oppression and brought low.
sentences.s621-s1: When the affairs of the land of Turan had been settled, the king's heart was freed from worry.
sentences.s621-s2: He commanded that a scribe come before him; he asked for a pen, with musk and Chinese silk.
sentences.s621-s3: The king wrote (or sent) a certain letter to Nersi concerning the fighting with the Turks and the affairs of the army.
sentences.s621-s4: He prefaced the letter with a hidden praise: 'From this servant to the Creator of the world.'
sentences.s621-s17: Now, after my letter, I will come with an army to fulfil the well-wisher's heart's desire.
sentences.s621-s18: The beasts, foam-flinging and swift‑footed, rushed off like a roaring cloud from their place.
sentences.s621-s19: When the letter drew near and arrived, the king's heart was borne away with joy.
sentences.s621-s20: The chief priest of priests came before him; every one of the heroes who sought battle (also came).
sentences.s621-s37: The Chaghani and the Chagli and the people of Balkh — the Bukharans, and the mobeds amid the clamour.
sentences.s621-s38: They went off with cries and, with the ritual in hand, praying, approached before the fire‑worshipper.
sentences.s621-s39: ‘That we are entirely the king’s servants,’ — to that very cry we have bowed our necks.
sentences.s621-s40: That same also, every year with cries and Sāv, came to the court; whoever it was, that radiance belonged to him.
sentences.s621-s21: A cry of joy rose from Iran; each one put his ear to the sound.
sentences.s621-s22: The hearts of the renowned were, because of the king's display, ever twisted — turned aside for fear of sin.
sentences.s621-s23: They went up to the mobed to ask pardon; all were terrified and became afraid of every ill.
sentences.s621-s24: For (they feared) that by crooked thought and the demon's command the khedive's heart might be carried away from the path of the world.
sentences.s621-s25: By that force of the army which produced this conviction that God will open the heavens.
sentences.s621-s26: It is wondrous that this conjecture exceeds even the opinion of a knowing, wise man.
sentences.s621-s27: When the messenger's reply, good or bad, arrives, we must write down this very apology.
sentences.s621-s28: But if, even so, some guilt left the nobles, would the famed king forgive (it)?
sentences.s621-s33: No one was chosen against the king — neither out of malicious enmity nor out of pain and hatred.
sentences.s621-s34: There was a certain preeminent man named Barzmehr; by going to him he opened the way of approach.
sentences.s621-s35: He came near the king of the world and revealed all the secrets from hiding.
sentences.s621-s36: By his words the king became pleased; thus a keen fire became smokeless.
sentences.s621-s29: Narsi accepted, "Now I will see what to do so that I may drive the king's hatred out of his heart."
sentences.s621-s30: Then he quickly wrote an answer to that letter and revealed in it both its good and its bad.
sentences.s621-s31: That the Iranians, on the one hand, pursue pain and suffering, and on the other, pursue homeland, children, and treasure.
sentences.s621-s32: They took the Khagan of China as their refuge, despairing of the aforementioned king.
